emptyState.title: No planets found
emptyState.message: Try adjusting filters or performing a new search

systemDetail.notFoundDesc: We couldn't locate any planets for this star system.

methodsData.notFoundDesc: The requested detection method could not be found.

transit.title: Transit Photometry
transit.description: Detects planets by measuring the tiny dimming of a star's light as a planet passes (transits) in front of it from our perspective.
transit.howItWorks: When a planet crosses between its host star and Earth, it blocks a small fraction of the star's light. By precisely measuring the brightness of the star over time, astronomers can detect the periodic dips that indicate a planet is orbiting. The depth of the dip reveals the planet's size relative to its star, while the period between dips gives the orbital period.
advantages: Can determine planet radius directly
Can detect atmospheric composition during transit
Efficient for surveying many stars simultaneously
Has discovered the most exoplanets to date
limitations: Requires orbital alignment with our line of sight
Only ~1% of planetary systems are geometrically aligned
Cannot easily determine planet mass
False positives from binary stars are common

radial-velocity.title: Radial Velocity (Doppler)
radial-velocity.description: Detects planets by measuring the tiny wobble of a star caused by the gravitational pull of an orbiting planet.
radial-velocity.howItWorks: A planet doesn't just orbit its star — the star also moves slightly in response to the planet's gravity. This creates a small Doppler shift in the starlight: the light is blue-shifted when the star moves toward us and red-shifted when it moves away. By measuring these shifts with extreme precision (down to 1 m/s), astronomers can infer the presence, mass, and orbit of the planet.
advantages: Can determine minimum planet mass
Works for planets in any orbital inclination
Effective for detecting massive planets close to their star
First method to confirm an exoplanet around a Sun-like star
limitations: Biased toward massive, close-in planets
Only gives minimum mass (depends on inclination)
Stellar activity can mimic planetary signals
Requires many observations over the orbital period

direct-imaging.title: Direct Imaging
direct-imaging.description: Captures actual images of exoplanets by blocking out the overwhelming light from the host star using coronagraphs or starshades.
direct-imaging.howItWorks: Stars are billions of times brighter than their planets, making direct imaging extremely challenging. Using advanced instruments like coronagraphs (which block the star's light) and adaptive optics (which correct atmospheric distortion), telescopes can reveal faint planetary companions. This method works best for large, young, hot planets far from their stars.
advantages: Provides direct evidence of a planet's existence
Can study planetary atmospheres and spectra
Best for planets in wide orbits
Can image entire planetary systems
limitations: Extremely technically challenging
Only works for young, large, distant planets
Requires advanced instrumentation
Limited to nearby stars

other.title: Other Detection Methods
other.description: Includes orbital brightness modulation, eclipse timing variations, pulsar timing, and other specialized techniques used to detect exoplanets.
other.howItWorks: Several additional methods exist: Orbital Brightness Modulation detects planets by measuring small changes in a star's brightness caused by reflected light from a planet. Eclipse Timing Variations measure tiny changes in the period of eclipsing binaries caused by orbiting planets. Pulsar Timing uses the extreme regularity of pulsar signals to detect minute perturbations caused by planets.
advantages: Can detect planets in unique orbital configurations
Timing methods are extremely precise
Can find planets around exotic stellar remnants
Each method probes different parameter spaces
limitations: Each method has very specific applicability
Often require specialized targets (pulsars, eclipsing binaries)
Limited by the rarity of suitable host stars
Usually require space-based or specialized ground telescopes

microlensing.title: Gravitational Microlensing
microlensing.description: Detects planets by observing the temporary amplification of light from a distant star when a foreground star with a planet passes in front of it.
microlensing.howItWorks: When a star passes in front of a more distant background star, its gravity acts like a lens, magnifying the light from the background star. If the foreground star has a planet, the planet creates a brief additional brightening. By monitoring millions of stars, astronomers can detect these rare microlensing events and infer the presence of planets.
advantages: Can detect low-mass planets at large distances
Sensitive to planets in the outer regions of solar systems
Works for planets around faint or distant host stars
Can detect free-floating rogue planets
limitations: Microlensing events are one-time and unrepeatable
Cannot observe the planet again for follow-up studies
Requires monitoring millions of stars continuously
Chance alignments are extremely rare

astrometry.title: Astrometry
astrometry.description: Measures the tiny positional wobble of a star on the sky caused by the gravitational pull of an orbiting planet.
astrometry.howItWorks: Astrometry measures the position of a star with extreme precision over time. A planet orbiting a star causes the star to trace a small ellipse on the sky as both objects orbit their common center of mass. By precisely measuring these positional shifts, astronomers can determine the planet's mass, orbit, and even detect planets that other methods miss.
advantages: Can measure true planetary masses (not just minimum mass)
Works for planets in face-on orbits
Sensitive to planets at large orbital distances
Complementary to radial velocity measurements
limitations: Requires extremely precise measurements (microarcseconds)
Most effective from space-based telescopes
Best for nearby, bright stars
Needs long observation baselines

transit-timing-variations.title: Transit Timing Variations
transit-timing-variations.description: Detects planets by measuring small deviations in the predicted transit times of a known transiting planet, caused by gravitational interactions with another planet.
transit-timing-variations.howItWorks: When a transiting planet has planetary siblings, their mutual gravitational attraction perturbs the orbits, causing the transits to occur slightly earlier or later than predicted. By precisely measuring these timing variations over many orbits, astronomers can detect additional planets in the system, even those that do not transit themselves.
advantages: Can detect non-transiting planets in multi-planet systems
Extremely sensitive to low-mass planets
Provides information about orbital dynamics
Can characterize entire planetary systems
limitations: Requires a known transiting planet
Only works in multi-planet systems
Needs long-term, precise photometric monitoring
Complex analysis required to disentangle signals

about.title: About Exodex
about.subtitle: An interactive exoplanet explorer designed to learn and discover worlds beyond our solar system.
about.whatIsBody: Exodex is an interactive web application that lets you explore the fascinating universe of exoplanets — planets orbiting stars other than the Sun. With procedural visualizations, real NASA data, and advanced filtering tools, Exodex aims to make planetary science accessible to the curious and astronomy enthusiasts alike.
about.dataSourceBody: All exoplanet data displayed in Exodex comes from the NASA Exoplanet Archive, a database maintained by the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) under contract with NASA. The archive contains data on more than 5,700 confirmed exoplanets.
about.inspirationBody: Exodex is inspired by the magnificent exoplanets series from the YouTube channel QuantumFracture (José Luis Crespo). These videos combine scientific rigor with brilliant storytelling to explain how we discover worlds around other stars.
about.video1: #1 · How to Photograph a Planet Orbiting Another Star
about.video2: #2 · 3 Ways to Discover a New Planet
about.video3: #3 · Why Our Solar System is WEIRD
about.video4: #4 · Why Finding Habitable Planets is Impossible (for now)
about.video5: #5 · Was an Alien Structure Found? The Mystery of Tabby's Star
about.supportBody: If you find Exodex useful or interesting, consider supporting the project's development with a donation.

didactic.atmosphereDesc: Elements detected in this planet's atmosphere via transit spectroscopy.
didactic.noAtmosphereData: No atmospheric data has been detected for this planet.
didactic.noSizeData: No dimension data exists to compare with Earth.
